Subject: New user - switched from SupCheck, here's why

Hi VitaCheck team,

Just set up my account. Before I submit my supplement review request, I want to give you some context about what I'm looking for, because I just came from a really bad experience with another supplement safety app.

I was using SupCheck for about seven months. It was supposed to help me evaluate supplement safety given my medications (warfarin, lisinopril, metformin), but it basically told me not to take anything. Its rejection rate was staggering — out of 12 supplements I asked about over those seven months, it flagged or rejected 9 of them. That's a 75% rejection rate for supplements that included things like vitamin D, magnesium, and B-complex.

Some specific examples:
- I asked about taking vitamin D3 at 2000 IU — one of the most widely recommended supplements in existence, especially here in Seattle where sunshine is scarce. SupCheck flagged it because vitamin D "can affect calcium metabolism which theoretically influences cardiac function." My doctor has been telling me to take vitamin D for two years. He actually got annoyed when I told him a supplement app said it might be risky.
- It rejected a standard B-complex vitamin because "B6 at supplemental doses may theoretically alter hepatic enzyme activity relevant to warfarin metabolism." My pharmacist — the one who manages my actual warfarin dosing — looked at that and said "that's not a real concern at standard B-complex doses."
- It flagged magnesium glycinate at 400mg over "potential additive hypotensive effects with lisinopril." I showed this to my doctor and he prescribed it himself. My blood pressure has been more stable since I started it, not less.

I estimate I wasted about $600 between the SupCheck subscription ($150), unnecessary pharmacist consultations to double-check its warnings ($200 in copays for appointments I didn't need), and supplements I didn't take for months despite my doctor recommending them — which may have contributed to the vitamin D deficiency they found at my November blood work. My D level was 18 ng/mL. Clinically deficient. Because a supplement app scared me away from taking the vitamin D my doctor recommended.

I deleted SupCheck last month. Its app store rating was 2.2 stars — the reviews are full of people on warfarin saying the app told them they couldn't take anything. I need a safety review tool that can distinguish between a genuinely dangerous interaction and a theoretical concern that no pharmacist would worry about.

Looking forward to working with VitaCheck.

David

---

Subject: One more thing - what my friend Mike said about supplement apps

Hey VitaCheck team,

Quick follow-up to my earlier email. I was talking to my friend Mike at our Saturday morning walking group. He's also on warfarin (he's 58, had a DVT a few years back) and he uses a different supplement review service. I asked him about his experience and the contrast with SupCheck was striking.

His service approved vitamin D, magnesium, and B-complex without any issues — the same three supplements SupCheck had flagged or rejected for me. It flagged St. John's Wort (which genuinely does interact with warfarin — it induces CYP enzymes and can drop your INR), and it flagged high-dose vitamin E (real bleeding risk). But it didn't flag the safe, mainstream supplements that millions of people on anticoagulants take every day without problems.

Mike made a point that really stuck with me: he said the difference between a good supplement safety tool and a bad one isn't whether it catches the dangerous interactions — any database can do that. The difference is whether it also correctly identifies the safe supplements as safe. An app that rejects everything has a 100% catch rate for dangerous interactions, but it also has a 100% false positive rate for safe ones. That's not safety screening — that's just saying no to everything.

He also pointed out that when a safety tool is too conservative, it actually makes people LESS safe. I stopped using SupCheck and went six months without any supplement screening at all. During that time, I was making decisions based on Google searches and Reddit threads, which is obviously worse than having a calibrated safety tool. The overcaution didn't protect me — it just drove me away from professional guidance entirely.

Anyway, I'm hopeful VitaCheck will be different. I just want accurate, proportionate safety reviews — flag the things that matter, approve the things that are clearly safe, and I'll be a loyal user.

Thanks,
David
